metadata.seed: Why do I get so down (depressed?) at the beginning of every semester? It may not be real depression but I do get down and gloomy during the beginning of every semester. I’m in college and have a full time job, so I’m keeping pretty busy. But when I do have a little time to myself, I just want to sit and stare at a wall. It’s not just me; my family has noticed that I act this way too. Why do I do this and is there anything I can do to help? I’m taking 100mg of sertraline already!

patient.query: Why do I get so down (depressed?) at the beginning of every semester? It may not be real depression but I do get down and gloomy during the beginning of every semester. I’m in college and have a full time job, so I’m keeping pretty busy. But when I do have a little time to myself, I just want to sit and stare at a wall. It’s not just me; my family has noticed that I act this way too. Why do I do this and is there anything I can do to help? I’m taking 100mg of sertraline already!

llm_response.response:
It's understandable to feel overwhelmed or low during the beginning of a new semester, especially considering your busy schedule with college and work. This feeling might be due to the stress and adjustment period that comes with starting something new, or perhaps it's related to seasonal affective disorder (SAD), which can cause feelings of depression during the fall and winter months.

Your current medication, sertraline, is an antidepressant commonly used to treat depression, anxiety, and SAD. However, it may take some time for the full effects to be noticed, and adjusting to a new semester could still cause temporary feelings of sadness or fatigue.

To help manage these feelings, consider setting realistic goals for yourself, prioritizing self-care, and maintaining open communication with your family and support network. Additionally, ensuring you get enough sleep, exercise, and sunlight can also be beneficial in managing stress and improving mood. If your symptoms persist or worsen, it may be helpful to discuss these concerns with your healthcare provider to determine if any adjustments to your treatment plan are necessary.
